content.text: The body is negotiable. The paperwork is not.

A changed body is bought, built, or owed. Splices are harvested rift-fauna tissue, professionally integrated. Wild grafts are whole organs from specific creatures, grafted intact. And a body that runs hot long enough gets changed whether anyone paid or not. All of it is grounded biology — the strange stays plausible — and all of it prices into the same caps and budget as every augment in the game (the price list and caps — see Trees Overview).
The Dial
Mutations run at one of three printed settings, declared for the table before they matter. All three are balanced by construction; the economy doesn't change underneath you.

rows: No mutations at the table. The Saturated threshold deals a scar-class Strain instead. Nothing else changes.
Splices purchasable at providers. Wild grafts exist as rare loot and quest material. Saturation-forced mutations active.

The full pipeline: a Carver harvests the organ, the preservation clock runs, a Street Doc performs, and bespoke grafts get built with the worksheet below — player-built, purchased, or found, as the table sets it.

content.label: DESIGN GAP
content.text: The OFF setting's swap — "the Saturated threshold deals a scar-class Strain instead" — is canon as written, but the Strain's mechanics are not printed in the rebuild docs.
Three Doors Into a Changed Body
Splices (bought): harvested tissue, professionally integrated. The purchase lane — and the top of the Carver's market.
Wild grafts (crafted or found): whole organs grafted intact. The free-form lane — see the worksheet below.
Saturation-forced (owed): hit the Saturated threshold and the bill collects — roll a mutation, now. Nobody chose this one; that's the point. (The threshold itself — see Charge & Saturation.)
Local or Systemic

Local grafts — hearts, limbs, organs — are somatic. They change your body and die with you. Systemic grafts — marrow, glands that feed the whole body — can leave the mutagenic bloodline fingerprint: rare, quiet, and heritable. A fingerprint surfaces in hiring databases and flags at dome gates — yours, and your children's. It is social texture, not horror; the catalog marks which entries carry it.

APPLICANT NOTICE: Biometric screening is a standard and painless part of onboarding. Applicants presenting heritable non-baseline signatures will be processed under the family-account schedule. We thank you in advance for your transparency.
The Procedure
Installation is the d20's second customer. The Fate Roll has exactly two uses in this game — dying, and this (the death use lives on The Staircase). For a splice or graft, the surgeon's table rolls:
Fate Roll: d20 + Grit bonus + provider modifier vs the graft's Risk DC. A natural 20 always integrates.
The Risk tiers reuse the named DC ladder, on the d20 (the ladder — see The Two Rolls).
Provider modifiers: street −1 · clinic +1 · corp +3 — with corp paperwork, which is its own price.
Blooming: if your Saturation has reached Blooming, roll twice and take the worse (the threshold — see Charge & Saturation).

rows: Meets or beats the Risk DC
Minor complication: a cosmetic tell, plus an extra Saturation-floor increase.
Major complication: a condition until treated, and the graft site takes an injury.
Rejection crisis: the graft is lost and the body takes a scar — it remembers.

content.label: DESIGN GAP
content.text: The minor complication's "extra Saturation floor" is canon, but the size of the increase is not printed in the rebuild docs.

What the odds feel like: at Grit +2, a Risk II splice at a clinic runs about 65% clean, 15% minor, 20% major, and 0% rejection; the same splice on the street runs 55/15/20/10. Competence and money move the same dial, about five points a step. A Risk IV graft at a street doc is a 40% rejection.
clean odds look great on paper. the paper is never the one on the table.
The Floor — What Every Mutation Costs

Every mutation raises your Saturation floor by 5. You can never detox below your floor. The biology is rift-touched now; the body lives permanently closer to its thresholds. Three mutations is a floor of 15 — halfway to nothing good before breakfast. (Thresholds and detox — see Charge & Saturation.)

Beyond the floor, every entry carries three lines: a benefit (priced on the augment price list — see Trees Overview), a cost (mandatory and mechanical), and a scanner line — what a dome checkpoint sees. Domes scan. A mutation is a paperwork problem before it is anything else.
The Splice Catalog (Market)

rows: +2 Armor HP on any worn shell (body conforms)
molt: one downtime per arc or −1 EV
voice never sounds quite right again
Climb at full speed; falls ≤2 bands cost nothing
+4 HP; injuries heal one downtime faster
flagged at every gate, and so are your children
+1 on contests vs patterns and Intrusion
your own Weave checks at disadvantage
reads as a dead zone — which is its own flag

content.label: DESIGN GAP
content.text: Market level makes splices "purchasable at providers," but the rebuild docs print no credit prices for the catalog — only Risk tiers and mandatory costs. Purchase pricing is unwritten.
Mutation Rolls at Saturated

When your Saturation crosses the Saturated threshold (see Charge & Saturation), you roll on the wild table immediately. No provider, no Fate Roll, no choice — this is the unpaid-debt lane, and the debt collects on its own schedule.

content.label: DESIGN GAP
content.text: The wild mutation table — what you actually roll on at Saturated, and what a forced result looks like — is named in the rebuild docs but never printed.
Wild Grafts — the Worksheet (Frontier)
At Market level, wild grafts turn up as rare loot and quest material. At Frontier, you build them. A graft comes from any creature, in five lines:
Donor capability: name the one thing the creature does that the organ carries. The creature's signature line is the menu.
Price the benefit on the augment price list (see Trees Overview). Caps apply; capped currencies come as +1 conditional, the way mods price them (see Mods).
Mandatory cost: one mechanical drawback in the same domain. The organ wants what the animal wanted — appetite, light, heat, motion.
Risk tier = the creature's tier. Systemic? Does it feed the whole body — marrow, glands? If yes: it carries the bloodline fingerprint line.
Scanner line: what a dome checkpoint sees.

Worked example — Char Hound heart (Risk II, local): benefit +1 RP — a reactive cardiovascular system; shares the RP cap, audited. Cost: the resting heart rate of a sprinting animal — double rations, and a Resilience Check vs Rattled when ambushed, because the heart answers before you do. Scanner: cardiac signature reads as fauna; checkpoint medics get interested.
the organ remembers being an animal. be polite to it.
The preservation clock from carving applies to every donor organ — the heart is worth more than the rest of the hound, for about six hours. (The harvest, the clock, and the market — see Carver.)

content.label: THE SCAR LAW'S COUSIN
content.text: Nothing in this catalog touches the Fate Roll's death math. The body you build can make you strange — never safe.
